Fern Polrrrcel PRACTrces C oMMrssroN
.128 J Street o Suite 620 e Sacramenro. CA 958 l.t-2329
1916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
March 25,2008
David Bauer
2150 River Plaza Dr., Suite 150
Sacramento, California 95 833
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-08-045
Dear Mr. Bauer:
This letter is in response to your,reqllest for advice regarding fhe new provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") I related to state officeholder accounts and
committees. You have requested advice on behalf of Senator Dennis Hollingsworth.
QUESTTON
A member of the Senate Staff will be leaving state service and several senators
plan to purchase a going-away gift to the staffer by pooling donations of $200 each from
their campaign comrnittees. May Senator Hollingsworth use his officeholder funds for
his $200 share of the gift?
ANALYSIS AND CONCLUSION
In analyzing questions pertaining to the use of officeholder funds, we apply a two-
part test.
o First, we determine if the expenditure of officeholder funds is associated with
holding the specific office for which the funds were raised (Secrion 85316(b) and
Regulation 1853 1.62).
I The Political Refirrm Act is contained in Governntent Code Sections tt I000 throu-eh 9 1014. All
statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections lti I l0 through Itt997 of Title 2 of the Califirrnia
Code of Regulations. AII regulatory ret'erences are kr Title 2. Division 6 of the Calitilrnia Cocle of
Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.


File No. 4-08-045
Page No. 2
o [f the expenditures are permissible under these provisions, we next determine if the
expenditures are permissible under the Act's "personal use" provisions in Sections
89510 - 89522.
A. Expenses Associated with Holdtng Office
Section 853 l6(b) provides
"[A]n elected state officer may accept contributions after the date
of the electionfor the purpose of pcrying expenses associated with holtting
the ffice provided that the contributions are not expended for any
contribution to any state or local committee. Contributions received
pursuant to this subdivision shall be deposited into a bank account
established solely for the pllrposes specified in this subdivision.,'
[Emphasis added.]
An elected officer's duties can include a wide array of activities. Therefore, a
determination of whether a particular activity is associated with holding that office
requires a case-by-case analysis. In conducting this analysis, we believe that so long as
the predominant purpose of the activity is reasonably related to the duties of the office,
the activity is "associated with holding the office." A Senator providing a gift to a Senate
employee would meet this standard.
B. Personal Use Law
The "personal use" provisions of the Act were designed to prevent candidates,
elected officials, and others who control the expenditure of campaign funds from
benefiting personally from their campaign activities. These rulei also apply to the use of
officeholder contributions.
Section 89512.5 provides that any expenditure by a committee must be, at a
minimum, reasonably related to a political, legislative, or govemmental purpose. In
addition, Section 89513 of the personal use law sets forth itandards "on""*ing specific
types of expenditures such as travel and gifts. subdivision (f) provides:
"(l) campaign funds shalr not be used to make personal gifts
unless the gift is directly related to a political, legislative, o, gor-*mental
pLlrpose. The refund of a campaign contribution does not constitute the
making of a gift.
_ 
"(2) Nothing in this section shall prohibit the use of campaign
funds to reimburse or otherwise compensate a public employee ior-
services rendered to a candidate or committee while on vacation, leave, or
otherwise outside of compensated public time.


File No. 4-08-045
Page No. 3
"(3) An election victory celebration or similar campaign event, or
gifts with a total atmulotive yalue of less than two hunclred fi.fty tlollars
($250) in a single year macle to an individuar employee, a committee
w'orker, or on emplol,ee of the elected rfficer's agency, are considerecl to
be directl-,* related to a political, legislative, or goventnental purpose.
For purposes of this paragraph, a gift to a member of a person,s immediate
family shall be deemed to be a gift to that person." [Emphasis added.]
You stated that the Senator wishes to use the officeholder campaign funds to
make a $200 gift to a Senate employee. Assuming the total cumulative ,il.,e of gifts the
Senator has made to the specific Senate employee is valued at less than $250 in isingle
year, the gifts are considered to be directly related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose and the Senator may therefore use his campaign funds to make the
$200 donation toward the gift.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
W. Wallace
stant General Counsel
Division
Jwwjgl
